worth_it_explanation: Floor pricing sets the price below which an impression goes unsold, so it controls the trade between fill rate and the value of each impression a publisher sends to TripleLift. A floor that leans entirely on automation, or one flat number applied across every format, country, and device, can leave revenue uncollected without anyone noticing. The check reviews pricing decisions a publisher already controls, in the TripleLift console for direct integrations or in the Prebid floors configuration for header-bidding setups, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete pricing decision the publisher can test.
intro: Floor pricing decides the minimum price a publisher will accept for an impression sold through TripleLift, which sets where the line falls between filling more impressions and protecting the value of each one. TripleLift carries native, branded-content, and connected TV (CTV) formats whose clearing prices differ from standard display, and it connects to publishers both through direct integrations and through the TripleLift Prebid adapter. This subcategory is about the floor strategy itself, the values chosen and how they are applied, rather than the wiring that delivers them. These checks confirm that the floor strategy is a deliberate, evidence-based choice rather than automation accepted by default, and that the floors reflect the price differences across the publisher's inventory and formats.

audit_checks.title: Dynamic floors are accepted without evaluating them against a fixed-floor baseline
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Dynamic floors hand the floor to an automated model that adjusts it per request to balance fill and value, which often raises revenue, but the model optimizes toward yield rather than toward a publisher's own strategy, such as protecting a premium native or branded-content price point. Accepting a dynamic floor without comparing it to a fixed-floor baseline leaves the choice untested, so a publisher cannot tell whether the automated floor is helping or clearing inventory below the price they meant to protect.

educate.base:
A publisher selling through TripleLift can set a floor as a fixed number it chooses or hand the floor to an automated model that adjusts it per request. In a header-bidding setup, the Prebid Price Floors Module supports both, with floors hard-coded at the ad-unit level or fetched in real time from a dynamic floor provider during the auction. In a direct TripleLift integration, the floor lives in the console rather than in Prebid, but the same choice applies. Dynamic floors often raise revenue because they react to demand the publisher cannot see in advance, and they save the work of maintaining a floor table by hand.

The risk is not that automation is wrong. The risk is accepting it without a comparison. A dynamic floor optimizes toward the objective it was built for, which is filling the impression at the best price the model can find, and that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy. A publisher protecting a premium native or branded-content placement, or holding a deliberate floor to keep a sponsorship rate intact, may find the dynamic floor clears impressions below the price they meant to protect. Without a baseline to measure against, the publisher has no way to see that happening.

This comparison is straightforward to run. A publisher can apply a dynamic floor to a slice of inventory, hold a fixed floor on a comparable slice, and read the revenue, fill rate, and average clearing price across both. The Prebid floors module supports this directly through its A/B testing schema, which samples more than one floor model at auction time so the two strategies run side by side on live traffic. That turns the floor choice into a measured result rather than a default that was never questioned.

A few signs point to a dynamic floor being accepted rather than evaluated:
  • A dynamic floor provider or automated floor model runs across all inventory with no fixed-floor comparison on record
  • No experiment or before-and-after read exists showing how the dynamic floor performed against a fixed baseline
  • The publisher cannot say why the dynamic option was chosen beyond it being easier than maintaining a floor table
You may want to confirm that the dynamic-versus-fixed choice was made on evidence rather than by default. One approach is to hold a fixed-floor baseline on part of the inventory, run the dynamic floor on the rest, and compare the two long enough to read a stable result.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, premium placements such as a homepage native unit or a branded-content slot often carry a price the publisher set on purpose to protect direct-sold value. You may want to keep a fixed-floor baseline on those units while testing a dynamic floor elsewhere, so the comparison shows whether automation respects the premium or clears below it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages can command higher prices than editorial pages because the audience is closer to a purchase. You may want to compare a dynamic floor against a fixed floor on the commercial pages specifically, rather than judging it on a blended average that the lower-value editorial inventory pulls down.

learn_more.source_title: Module - Price Floors for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's detailed floor configuration documentation for direct console integrations is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether floors are set as fixed values or handed to a dynamic floor model, and look for any test result or before-and-after read that compares them.

prebid_config.label: Prebid floors configuration
steps: Open the Prebid configuration and find the price floors module setup, then note whether floors are hard-coded at the ad-unit level or fetched from a dynamic floor provider endpoint.
Check whether the floors module uses the A/B testing schema to run more than one floor model at once, which would mean a comparison is already in place.
Record which slices of inventory run on a dynamic floor and which, if any, run on a fixed floor that could serve as a comparison baseline.

publisher_reporting.label: Publisher revenue reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the inventory or ad unit, with revenue, impressions, fill rate, and average clearing price.
Compare a slice running a dynamic floor against a comparable slice on a fixed floor, if one exists, to see whether the dynamic floor raised revenue or cleared at a lower price.
Ask the publisher whether any experiment or before-and-after comparison was run, and record what the result showed and when it was measured.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per slice of inventory, whether the floor is fixed or dynamic, and whether any comparison between a dynamic floor and a fixed baseline exists with a result on record. A setup where the dynamic-versus-fixed choice is deliberate and backed by a comparison passes. A setup where dynamic floors are applied across inventory with no fixed-floor comparison and no stated reason for the choice is a fail recorded with the inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the floor type for each slice of inventory and whether a dynamic-versus-fixed comparison exists above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs a dynamic floor through TripleLift without a fixed-floor comparison on record, so the choice between an automated floor and a fixed floor has not been tested. A dynamic floor sets the price to fill the impression at the best price the model can find, which often helps, but that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy, such as protecting a premium native or branded-content price point, and without a baseline the difference goes unmeasured. {{voice_recommend}} holding a fixed-floor baseline on a slice of comparable inventory, running the dynamic floor on the rest, and comparing revenue, fill rate, and average clearing price across both before settling on a floor strategy. The Prebid floors module supports this side-by-side test directly through its A/B testing schema. After the comparison, keep whichever floor the data supports for each slice of inventory.

audit_checks.title: Floors are not segmented by format, geography, or device
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A single flat floor across all inventory ignores that native, branded-content, CTV, and standard display formats clear at different prices, and that geography and device move the clearing price further. A flat floor either underprices the strong inventory, leaving revenue uncollected, or overprices the weak inventory, leaving impressions unsold, so one number cannot serve inventory whose value varies along several lines at once.

educate.base:
A floor price works best when it matches what the impression is worth, and impressions are not worth the same across a publisher's TripleLift inventory. Format is the first divider. A native or branded-content unit, which TripleLift built its business around, clears at a different price than a standard display banner, and a connected TV (CTV) impression clears differently again. Geography moves the price further because traffic from a high-spending market clears higher than traffic from a market where advertiser demand is thin. Device adds another line since desktop, mobile web, and in-app inventory each carry their own demand and their own typical clearing price. A floor set as one flat number across all of this cannot fit inventory whose value varies along every one of those lines.

The cost of a flat floor cuts both ways. Set it to the average and it sits below the strong inventory, so impressions that could have cleared higher clear at the floor and the difference is left uncollected. Set it higher to protect the strong inventory and it sits above the weak inventory, so impressions that would have sold at a lower price go unsold instead. Either way, one number serves the middle and misprices the ends.

The Prebid Price Floors Module lets a publisher define floor rules against the dimensions that move clearing price, with a schema that matches on fields such as media type, size, ad unit, and domain, so a publisher can set different floors for different formats and placements rather than one floor for everything. In a direct TripleLift integration, the console carries the equivalent controls. The audit question is whether the floors in place reflect those value differences or whether a single flat floor is doing work that several segmented floors should do.

A few signs point to floors that are not segmented:
  • One floor value covers all inventory, with no breakout by format, geography, or device
  • Reporting shows average clearing prices that differ widely across native, display, and CTV formats while the floor stays the same for all of them
  • Premium native or branded-content placements clear at or near the same floor as standard display, which suggests the floor is not protecting their value
You may want to confirm that the floors reflect the price differences the inventory actually shows. One approach is to read average clearing price by format, geography, and device, then set floors that follow those differences rather than a single number across the board.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a native unit in a high-engagement section usually clears higher than a standard banner in an article footer, and audience from high-demand markets clears higher than audience from thin ones. You may want to set floors by format and geography so the premium native inventory holds its value while the lower-value display inventory keeps a floor low enough to stay filled.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages reach an audience closer to a purchase and clear higher than editorial pages, and device matters because mobile shoppers and desktop shoppers carry different demand. You may want to segment floors so the commercial, higher-intent inventory carries a higher floor than the editorial inventory rather than sharing one number.

learn_more.source_title: Module - Price Floors for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's detailed floor configuration documentation for direct console integrations is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the floor values across formats, geographies, and device categories to see whether they are differentiated or whether one flat floor covers everything.

prebid_config.label: Prebid floors configuration
steps: Open the Prebid price floors configuration and read the floor rules, then note which schema fields the rules match on, for example media type, size, ad unit, or domain.
Record whether the floor values differ across format, placement, geography, and device, or whether a single floor applies across all inventory with no breakout.
For a direct TripleLift integration, check the console floor settings for the same breakout rather than the Prebid configuration.

publisher_reporting.label: Publisher revenue reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by format, then by country, then by device category, with average clearing price and fill rate.
Compare the average clearing price across those dimensions to see how much it varies between strong and weak inventory.
Note any case where clearing prices differ widely but the floor stays the same, which points to inventory that a segmented floor could price more accurately.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the floor values across formats, geographies, and device categories alongside the average clearing price for each. Floors that follow the value differences the reporting shows, with higher floors on inventory that clears higher and lower floors on inventory that clears lower, pass. A single flat floor applied across inventory whose clearing prices differ widely is a fail recorded with the dimensions where the floor and the value diverge.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the floor values and the average clearing price by format, geography, and device above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} applies a single flat floor across TripleLift inventory whose value differs by format, geography, and device. A flat floor either underprices the strong inventory, such as native and branded-content placements, leaving revenue uncollected, or overprices the weak inventory, leaving impressions unsold, so one number cannot fit inventory that clears at different prices. {{voice_recommend}} reading the average clearing price by format, geography, and device, then setting floor rules, in the Prebid floors module for header-bidding setups or the TripleLift console for direct integrations, that carry higher floors on the inventory that clears higher and lower floors on the inventory that clears lower. After the change, recheck fill rate and average clearing price across the segments to confirm the floors fit the value of each.
